query: Philadelphia chromosome composition and involved chromosomes

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Clinical Significance -- PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE:
retrieved_docs.content: The status of the Philadelphia chromosome is associated with substantially different prognoses in patients of different leukemic phenotypes. The presence of the Ph chromosome has also correlated with a higher risk of concurrent genomic abnormalities. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Philadelphia chromosome (Ph), named after the city where it was first described, was described for the first time by Nowell and Hungerford in 1960, in two patients who had lost the long arm of chromosome 22. [1] [2] [3] As the Giemsa staining improved over the decade, Rowley showed that this truncated chromosome was generated as a result of the translocation of the long arm of chromosomes 9 and 22. [2] [3] [4] The presence of the abnormal Ph chromosome in cells of myeloid, megakaryocytic, along with erythroid lineages pointed to the origination of the disorder in a pluripotential stem cell. [2] Philadelphia chromosome is the hallmark of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) along with some other leukemias including acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) (mostly B cell ALL, rarely T cell ALL), acute myeloid leukemia (AML), chronic neutrophilic leukemia (CNL), and mixed phenotype acute leukemia (MPAL). [4] [5] [6] [7] [8] [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: As a result of different breakpoints in the BCR gene, that occur over quite a short DNA stretch of 5 -6 kilobases in the middle of the gene, three different BCR/ABL1 proteins are formed namely P190, P210, P230 which are usually associated with ALL, CML and CNL respectively. The numeral value represents the kDa size of the hybrid protein. There have been some reports showing correlations of P190 with CML, P210 with ALL, and P190 or P210 with AML. P230is formed by the fusion of the ABL1 gene with almost the entire BCR gene leading to the production of a 230-kDa protein. [2] It is the molecular diagnostic marker for neutrophilic-chronic myeloid leukemia (CML-N). [4] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: MAPK/ERK (RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK) pathway : This pathway is involved in signal transmission from cell surface receptors to nuclear factors involved in transcription. Osteopontin (OPN), which overexpresses in BCR/ABL1, uses this pathway to maintain a leukemic microenvironment. It, in turn, promotes and maintains the proliferation and survival of leukemic cells as well as plays a role in cell differentiation and apoptosis. [4] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: MPAL: Data collected from patients diagnosed with MPAL shows that the Ph chromosome is the most frequent aberrant cytogenetic abnormality, which has led to the recognition of Ph+MPAL as a distinct disease entity with a prevalence of less than 1% of all acute leukemias. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: The domains from BCR include: CC domain Ser/Thr kinase domain (contains a docking site Y177) Rho/GEF kinase domain The domains from ABL1 include: SH domain (SH1/SH2) Proline-rich domain DNA & actin-binding domains

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There have been reports of detection of the chromosomal abnormality rarely in normal healthy people. [2] Recently, a Philadelphia like ALL (Ph-like ALL) has been identified, which lacks the novel BCR-ABL1 translocation. [32] CML: The Ph chromosome is the diagnostic feature of CML. CML has an incidence of 50 per million per year and accounts for approximately 7 to 15% of the leukemias in the adult population. [2] [33] ALL: Philadelphia chromosome has been detected in 11 to 29 % of patients with ALL, although it is rarer with a prevalence of 1 to 3% in childhood ALL. [34] [35] AML: In patients with AML, the chromosomal abnormality has a reported incidence of less than 1.5%. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: C/EBP -mediated differentiation : CCAAT/enhancer-binding proteins (C/EBPs) are a type of transcription factors that are involved in the regulation of normal maturation of common myeloid cells to granulocyte-monocyte progenitor cells. This pathway is suppressed by the BCR/ABL1 oncogene. [4] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: This oncogene leads to constitutive activation of tyrosine kinase, which is responsible for more proliferation and better viability of the myeloid lineage cells. [4] The translocation results in decreased requirements for growth factors and provides freedom from their regulatory control. [2] This oncogene also affects cellular differentiation and apoptosis. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Clinical Significance -- THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS:
retrieved_docs.content: In patients diagnosed with the chronic phase of CML, risk calculations are performed before initiation of therapy. For this purpose, two different scoring systems are used. [31] One is the Sokal risk calculation, which requires the age of the patient, platelet count, the percentage of myeloblasts found in the peripheral blood and size of the spleen, whereas the Hasford risk calculation utilizes the same variables as Sokal calculator except for the percentage of myeloblasts it uses the percentage of blasts, basophils, and eosinophils. [42] [43] [42] For patients that have an intermediate or higher risk score, the recommendation is to consider the second-generation TKIs as the first line. [30] [40] [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: CONCURRENT GENETIC ABNORMALITIES :

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Clinical Significance -- PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE:
retrieved_docs.content: In patients of CML, the location of the breakpoint in the BCR gene has shown an important association with prognosis. CML patients with a double Philadelphia chromosome are associated with a worse prognosis than those with a single Philadelphia chromosome. [46] The presence of concurrent genetic abnormalities is associated with a worse prognosis than in the blastic phase of CML. [2] ALL patients, both adults, as well as children who are Ph+ive, have shown a poor prognosis in comparison to those who are Ph -ive. [8] Patients with Ph+ive MPAL have shown a worse outcome in comparison to other leukemic phenotypes. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to CML, Philadelphia chromosome has shown an association with different leukemias including Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) [4] [5] Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL)  (mostly B cell ALL, rarely T cell ALL) [6] [7] [8] Chronic Neutrophilic Leukemia (CNL) [9] Mixed Phenotype Acute Leukemia (MPAL) [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: The CC domain and Y177 of BCR are essential for the activation of the ABL1 gene and have shown to be closely associated with sensitivity to tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI). The Rho/GEF kinase domain plays a vital role in activating differentiation in the protooncogene induced leukemogenesis. [4] [13] The SH domain of ABL1 controls the gene’s activation and deactivation, and hence this domain is an important site for pharmacologic intervention. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Studies have shown that primary or acquired resistance to treatment as well as relapse, can be attributed to different mutations. Rearrangements in the BCR gene can help in the prediction of response to therapy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The normal ABL Tyrosine Kinase (TK) is responsible for the activation of signal pathways that lead to increased proliferation, better viability, and changed migration as well as homing. Normally these pathways are under close regulation by different hematopoietic growth factors. [2] The ABL1 protein is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase, and it exists throughout the hematopoietic development, however myeloid maturation results in its declining levels. [4] The normal BCR gene is responsible for the oxidative burst in neutrophils.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and dasatinib anhydrous are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a class of targeted therapies that block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase activity implicated in certain leukemias. By inhibiting this oncogenic kinase, both the standard and anhydrous formulations of dasatinib help to control malignant cell proliferation and are used in the management of chronic myeloid leukemia and Philadelphia chromosome‑positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Recognizing dasatinib and its anhydrous form as BCR‑ABL inhibitors underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in precision oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings” includes specific abnormal results such as a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) and an abnormal chromosome analysis (تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي). These particular findings are considered sub‑categories of the broader group of nonspecific abnormal laboratory results, indicating that when such tests return these outcomes, they represent distinct types of abnormal cytological, histological, immunological, or DNA evidence that require further clinical interpretation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2697
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “cancer; other and unspecified primary” includes malignant blood tumors (الأورام الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) as a member of this group, indicating that these hematologic malignancies are categorized under cancers of other or unspecified primary origin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib, also known as imatinib mesylate, is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor; it belongs to the class of agents that target the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase and is used therapeutically to inhibit this oncogenic driver in relevant hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2801
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a specialized classification system that incorporates the concept of bcr-abl fusion proteins as well as the concept of glukokortikoidy (glucocorticoids). By including bcr-abl fusion proteins, the terminology acknowledges the relevance of these oncogenic fusion proteins—commonly associated with chronic myeloid leukemia and other hematologic malignancies—as important biomarkers for disease detection, prognosis, and targeted therapy. The inclusion of glukokortikoidy reflects the significance of glucocorticoids, a class of steroid hormones that are widely used in clinical practice for their anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and can also serve as biomarkers for therapeutic response and endocrine function. Together, these concepts illustrate the breadth of CTRP Biomarker Terminology in covering both molecular oncogenic markers and pharmacologically active hormone classes relevant to patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1361
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a classification framework that encompasses a diverse set of biomarker concepts, including the bcr‑abl fusion proteins, human factor VIII, and the sex steroid hormones. By grouping these distinct molecular entities—oncogenic fusion proteins (bcr‑abl), a coagulation factor critical for hemostasis (factor VIII), and endogenous steroid hormones that regulate reproductive and metabolic functions—CTRP Biomarker Terminology provides a structured reference for researchers and clinicians to identify and discuss biomarkers across oncology, hematology, and endocrinology. This inclusive categorization facilitates interdisciplinary communication and supports integrated approaches to diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic decision‑making that involve these varied biomarker types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings represent a broad category of atypical laboratory results, and within this category the specific finding تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis) is included as a child or sub‑type, indicating that abnormal chromosome analysis is one of the nonspecific abnormal test results encompassed by this broader classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation of فقر دم فانكوني (Fanconi anemia), indicating that the clinical and laboratory findings associated with this genetic disorder are considered abnormal. Fanconi anemia is a hereditary condition characterized by bone‑marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and a heightened risk of malignancies, and the descriptor “Abnormal” reflects the pathological nature of its presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: BCR-ABL Inhibitor is a class of targeted therapy that includes ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib. Ponatinib and its hydrochloride form, as well as asciminib, are members of the BCR-ABL Inhibitor class and are employed in the treatment of أبيضاض الدم النقوي, a myeloid leukemia. These agents act by inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase, thereby controlling disease progression. Their shared mechanism and therapeutic indication underscore the importance of BCR‑ABL inhibition in managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 92
retrieved_docs.content: bone and joint disease, is linked to Stiffler syndrome. Stomach cancer (سرطان المعدة) is clinically associated with leukopenia, underscoring a hematologic manifestation of malignancy. Overall, the community’s knowledge maps metabolic, infectious, rheumatologic, gastrointestinal, oncologic, gynecologic, musculoskeletal, psychiatric, and genetic conditions to their shared clinical features and to a set of anti‑inflammatory and other therapeutic agents, providing an integrated framework for diagnosis and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and imatinib mesylát are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي, a form of lymphoid leukemia. Both drugs target abnormal tyrosine‑kinase activity characteristic of this hematologic malignancy, providing a targeted approach to disease control and highlighting the role of kinase inhibitors in modern leukemia treatment.
